Good afternoon everybody. Welcome to the last Diamond Call of 2013. I have a couple of housekeeping things and then in just a couple of minutes we will have probably a three-way competition although we are missing one of our competitors at the moment. But so we will either have a three-way competition or they will benefit by his absence and we will have a two-way competition. So we have even two or three very tough competitors and with great stories to tell and great things to show that you'll be able to go see. And then somebody will be going to Winner's Weekend and somebody will be, as they say, on American Idol. And somebody will be going home. So just a couple of things. One, I want to remind you that our first Diamond Call of next year in January is all about lead generation. A subject that should be near and dear to everybody's heart as the lifeblood of their businesses will be joined by Darren Spindler who amongst other things has built kidsbullfree.com. To two million plus customers and those are things or two about lead generation and specifically online lead generation. So that will be a great discussion and you will have an opportunity of course for Q&A. And in a sense you'll be getting a little preview of Super Conference as well because Darren will be making an in-depth presentation at Super Conference. And he's one of several presenters for Super Conference of next year who are what I call real practitioners as opposed to experts selling expertise. So Darren will be showing what he's really using in his real businesses. Matt Buchel from Down Under will be showing what he's really using in his businesses. And my longtime client Craig Brockter who for well over a decade has been one of the top real estate agents in the entire world. And in the top 10 and in the top 20 in the remax world even though in a relatively small market Toronto. And Craig has been using our kind of marketing since before technology started to make it easier and more functional. I often tell the story of Craig having a bazillion telephone answering machines in his basement each one with a different recorded message on it. And Catherine having to go down and strip the messages off several times during the day. Obviously all that's gotten a lot easier. So we will have a number of people at Super Conference who are presenting what they did, what they do. In some cases also what they teach. But predominantly what they did and what they do to build great businesses and to do good consumer and good B2B marketing. And I know there is a Super Conference registration deadline coming up which now either slips my mind or I didn't know it in the first place. But it is undoubtedly eminent. It usually ends before the end of the month. And the Super Conference information is I believe up and available online at GKIC.com. The other deadline after I remind you about is that there is a December 22nd deadline followed by a fee increase in place for the Dan only events in June. And so now we have our Diamond Marketer of the Year finalists with us. And each has a great story to tell. And I will be listening just as closely as you. And at the end of the call I will get some input from three people who are listening as well. One at GKIC, two others. I will be getting three faxes here and be announcing the winner right here and now on the call. As I told them, I say in advance that as they say at the Oscars, it's an honor just to be nominated. And it will undoubtedly be difficult to make a choice. I hope that the three people who I have invagaled as judges, all three of whom for obvious reasons, since Dwight is involved, wish to remain anonymous. But nobody wants to get beaten up at the next conference. Hopefully they will make the decision and I will not have to be a deciding vote. But in any case, we will have a winner above all winners by the end of this time and you will hopefully not only be inspired but will pick up an idea or two that you can use. So I know we have Richard Jacobs on the line and I know we have Dwight Woods on the line. Has Vance Morris joined us? I hear silence so that means no. So at the moment we have two competitors and the random draw that was done about order had Vance first. So we'll just go by alphabet and that means Richard, you get to go first. And you have roughly 15 minutes. Remember that hopefully you have a website to give out where people can see some things, either now or subsequent to the call or both. And some of what you have to tell us will have to do with specific application of things gained by being a Diamond Member and be hopefully even by one or more of these calls. So let's start with who you are, what you do, where you came from and we'll go from there. Okay. Can you hear me okay, Dan? We can hear you terrific. Great. Alright, this is Richard Jacobs. My niche area is marketing to attorneys, small firms, either solar practitioners or one, two or three attorney firms. And I've been in it for about four years. And first of all, I want to say this is great to be on the call. I really appreciate the best of this. It's exciting and a little bit nerve-wracking. But anyway, I started working in this industry because I guess I thought that, you know, attorneys, they had a good amount of money. And it will be rewarding to work with them, as you might guess and may laugh at. It's very, very tough to work with them. They're very suspicious people. Yes, they do have money, but they seem to get constantly hounded for it and they're not easy to market to. They get called by people every single day pitching, you know, SEO, website work, everything under the sun. So they have gatekeepers. Again, they're very suspicious. They take a long time to make a decision. So here I am in this market trying to learn how to get through to them and to get clients. And in the meantime, I had started listening to Dan Kennedy and GKIC stuff. And my first big breakthrough came was a super conference 2012. I went there and at the conference, they had a professionals day. So I met the dentist. I met podiatrist. I met all these other professionals. And I saw that they all had written books. I thought this was kind of odd. You know, here's a dentist that had written a book and I asked him, how does it work for you? He says, well, I give it out to clients and it's great. It really helps them get clients. And then I met a CPA and he also had a book. And then I met, you know, three or four other professionals. I think Matt Zagula was there. He's a financial guy and he has a book he gives out to people. So the idea started incubating in my mind. I guess I have to do the same thing to try to get through to my guys. So it took me a while, but fast forward, you know, a little over a year from there. And in the meantime, I'm hearing Dan talk about, you know, you have to establish yourself as the implied expert and the authority in your field. And he's, you know, talking about all the ways to do that. And this book had been on my mind, on my mind. And I finally decided, all right, I've got to do it. And the way I did it is pretty kind of unusual. I didn't want to sit there and write it all out like, you know, Dan does in a weekend. So what I chose to do was speak it. I drive in the car every day for about an hour going on various errands. So I decided to record myself speaking the book. And it took me three hours over several days, but I got the whole thing spoken. And then I had it transcribed and edited. I did the editing myself. And now I had a 180-page book. I call it Secrets of Attorney Marketing Law School Dares Not Teach. And I got it up on Amazon Kindle. And I started using it as part of my trust process. At first, you know, I didn't know what to do, but after a while, I figured out to send it out only to people that, you know, had raised their hands and stood in interest in some of my other material. And the book started to work wonders. I would get calls from attorneys saying, you know, I read your whole book. I love it. And then after we talked about working together. And it literally changed my entire business by doing this. It was if the people that I was chasing before now were attracted to me. It was crazy. You could literally hear it in their voices when they spoke to me and when they emailed me. And I realized, wow, this stuff really does work. I didn't realize it until I did it, but now I see that it does work. I don't know about why is this working like this? And I realized the book is not a sales pitch. It's an actual physical thing that kind of flies under the radar and it flies under people's defenses. And they can consume the material and it conveys expertise upon me and, you know, a bit of celebrity and all these things Dan talks about. And it really does work. It transforms this formerly suspicious and mean and, you know, begrudging attorney that I was trying so desperately to get into someone that's calling me. So it literally, if I could sum it up, it turned my business from chasing to attraction in a matter of months by using it. And the more I use it, the more it's happening. Before I was struggling and I was, you know, up to, you know, let's say 20, 25 attorneys and I would get a few, I'd lose a few, get a few, lose a few. Now I'm moving up past 45 and I'm in a very comfortable spot. My business is growing and I'm getting multiple calls sometimes a day. So this one thing, again, was just, it was just a huge instrumental fundamental shift in my business. You know, yes, I saw it. Yes, I saw everyone doing it. And where I saw it again was at, you know, the Super Conference and I've seen it since. So that's kind of like the summary of what's happening. I've taken it a step further from there. In speaking with, you know, some members of conferences, they told me, you know, now that you have your book, that's great, but, you know, here's some more ways to use it. So, you know, I won't go into that now, but one particular unusual thing that I've done with it is in creating the book, instead of writing it, I decided to speak it. And I brought that concept out now to my attorney clients. Every month I get on the phone or I have someone get on the phone with my attorney clients and we have them speak content for their websites. And that's helped me improve the results I deliver to them because now we've got a really leveraged way to get a lot of information out of them and to put it on their website. In addition now, I'm helping other people create books through what I call my speak-of-book process. We get on the phone for one hour, we do a Q&A call that gets transcribed, edited, and literally turned into a book. And that's become a side business for me that's really starting to grow. So, you know, my colleague talks about piggybacking one business on top of the other. Well, this has become a giant piggyback that may soon eclipse the original business because this, you know, my speak-of-book process is working really well, it's efficient, very leveraged, and I'm starting to talk to CPAs and people in other fields. So, you know, these are the teachings from Dan and GK. You know, it took me maybe a month, you know, six months or a year to really percolate my mind and use, but they really work. It's kind of crazy. And maybe a statistic that might make you laugh is, so my book is on Amazon and I have sold, I don't know, maybe 50 copies, made 200 bucks in royalties. But by handing out the book and myself, it's not all in mailing and out to people. I've made well over 40,000 in the past few months from doing that. So it's just kind of crazy to see, you know, like, they have another thing that Dan talks to them, teaches is that you're not going to make money selling your book, but look what's making all the money is doing this other thing, using it in the sales process. So I also want to use this time as a thank you to Dan and his teachings and GKIC and to all the members. The deeper I go into the GKIC, you know, going from gold to diamond, and now I'm going to be looking at maybe, you know, platinum, the more I learn, the more I'm around people that are utilizing these techniques profiting from them, and it just makes it accessible in my mind that, yes, I can do it, and yes, it works. So I guess that's the summary. Well, I want to congratulate you first of all. I do want to make a point for everybody that, you know, you are certainly not alone in requiring some sort of gestation period and repetition from the time you first see something being done, first see an idea in our world, and then, you know, have to sort of see it again and see it again until it finally kind of clicks and you act on it. And that's why people who hang around for long periods of time and are at every conference and sort of do everything, continue to benefit. It's not necessarily that this time around they're seeing something brand new that has never been shown or talked about before. It's just that at this point in time they're ready for it to click, and the cumulative exposure to it up to that point has gotten them ready to have it click. And everybody ought to really be aware of the fact that that happens. It happens more than, oh, see something and immediately grab it and run with it. And it's simply why you really need to stay connected. The experience you've had with it changing the dynamic of the sales process for all of the reasons you named is something that most people do experience when they start to use information media and authority media in order to market themselves and their businesses. And the second business on the back of the first is also worthy of praise. It's an opportunity a lot of people don't follow through on, but that evolves and exists. So congratulations. Do you have a place for people to see what you're doing? Yeah, I can give the website address if you're ready. Okay, so I'm going to give you the name. So it's speakeasymarketingink.com. And if you want to see the books, you'll go to speakeasymarketingink.com slash 40Kbook40KBOOK. Cool. All right, Richard, good luck to you. Thank you. And I understand Vance has joined us. I have. Well, you were first by random draw, so you now get to go second by random draw. Oh my goodness. I did not know how I could respond to the call. So you are up and hopefully you just heard Richard, and we would like you to do the same thing. Share your report. Give us a handle on your business and then share your report of what's been going on, what's worked, what, as a diamond member, has been important and useful to you that you have translated into results. And you have about 15 minutes. Great, Dan. Thanks very much for choosing me to be on the call. I certainly appreciate that. My story goes back a number of years to about 2007, and I hate to go too far into recent history, but to give you an idea of where I was and where I am now, it's important to give a little historical. Back in 2007, with the impending financial doom right around the corner, I made the incredible choice to go into business for myself. After two layoffs in corporate world, I was getting a little bit tired of it. So I said, you know, I'm going to do this myself. So 2007 bought myself a carpet cleaning franchise, and then all hell broke loose with the economy. So I didn't know any better. I figured I was just one of the lucky ones because I started to do really well. I had 10 years of Disney experience, and I was actually providing levels of service in an area that is not known for high levels of service. There are many shysters, many folks showing their backsides, showing up in jeans, smelling like smoke, and that was not the way I was running the business. So I had already decided that I was going to be offering a premium position. Within a couple of months, my wife had a significant medical issue, and she had what was called an AVM, which is an arteriovenous malformation. And it was a blood vessel just decided to explode in her head. Now, 32 years old stuff is not supposed to be exploding inside of your head. She was probably the youngest person in the ICU by about 50 years. Not to make light of it, but I make things explode in people's heads, and their heads explode on a regular basis, regardless of their age. Well, but... Yeah, I tend to do that with my wife currently, although she has not had a relapse. Thank goodness. Well, that's good. So our salaries had gotten to the point of she was now unemployed and unemployable, and I was reliant on a business that had just started two months ago. So essentially, we were at zero. And we slogged through it all. I paid attention to what our franchisor said was great marketing, which in my mind is what's called pretty picture marketing. You slap up a picture of a puppy on top of some dirty carpet, and then you show the clean puppy in the clean carpet, and you should have a lot of business rolling in. Well, I spent a lot of money doing that kind of stuff and not seeing much of a return, if at all, usually in the 1.1, 1.2 to 1 range. A few years ago, I had heard of Dan Kennedy through a couple of friends, and I went to the Washington, D.C. boot camp, and I was in about 30 minutes going to the boot camp. Dave D. was there, and Darcy was there, and I said, you know, whatever the heck it is they're selling at the end of this thing, I'm buying it because I was absolutely hooked. I learned more in the 12 hours I was at the D.C. boot camp than I did in three or four years of business ownership through the franchise. I just went through what I call a massive transformation. I bought magnetic marketing. I got all of the stuff. I mean, that's all I listened to in the car for probably, you know, a year straight. My kids were driving. Dad turned that off already. Actually, I think my oldest son can probably recite just number five verbatim if you asked him to. But the key thing that I found in the marketing was that there was not just one magic pill. It wasn't just a sales letter. It wasn't just a bank bag mailer. It wasn't just how you packaged your pricing. It was all of that. And you have to do, and I know Dan, you talk about complex sales funnels and things like that, and it's true. For a carpet cleaner, I've probably got about 19 to 25 different marketing funnels working at any one time. I did the arrive like no one else. We show up. We don't really have a Chakanaw package. We have a Chakanaw arrival. When we arrive at a client's house, we're laying down the red carpet. We're making a big show of wiping our feet. And then we hand our client the gift. And it's nothing great, but it's a little bad. A bottle of our spot remover, a little bag of Mrs. Fields cookies with a little note from me. And we're just automatically setting folks up for some reciprocity. We're going to give them a gift. We know we're going to get something in return. Before I started Dan style marketing, I was just slogging through business. It was horrendous. My job average was like $250, which actually that's what the franchise said it should be at, but I wasn't making any money. So I implemented the Giorgio three step letter. It was one of the first things I did and converted it into a carpet cleaning letter. And I saw some results from that. I said, well, golly, this direct response stuff actually works. So I started doing some more sequential mailers designed for different niches within my industry. We did commercial and I did the dollar bill mailer and then followed that up with a bank bag mailer trying to get, you know, just as you say, get things opened and read. I sent out about 20 of that sequence, the dollar bill mailer and the bank bag mailer. I sent about 20 of those out a month. Typically I get anywhere between seven and 10 appointments for commercial estimates. And once I get in there, I can convert anything. So I started writing books. I actually have four books now for the carpet cleaning world. One on Oriental Rugs, one on Inter-Air Quality, one on, you know, carpet cleaning for pet owners. And that was the huge one. Talk about the biggest aha moment I think I had was going after specific niches with specific messages and going after the pet owners, there's a lot of pee and poo out there. Without pets, I'd be out of business. But having direct marketing specifically for those niches was just unbelievable, absolutely unbelievable. And I found that as I was doing these, that one, my ROI was really starting to go up. And two, I was really starting to attract the clients that I wanted. And within the course of about 12 months, I was able to take my job average, which was at $250 a job, to north of $350 a job. So I don't know how many percent up that is, but it was significant. I was working with clients that liked what we did and would pay implementing the newsletter. And I think that's a good gracious, I can't believe I wasn't doing a newsletter years ago. I can't say that I get a one-to-one or a 1.5 return on the newsletter, but I know anecdotally that the redemption of coupons and offers has significantly dropped. And the only thing I did differently was implement the newsletter. Just reduced all of the little piddly discount things that I used to do 10%, 12%, 17% off. The newsletter was incredible. And then I changed the newsletter. And there's a guy up in Maine who does what he calls big-ass mailings. And I actually turned my eight-page newsletter into a giant mailing, which is actually 13 inches by 15 inches, mails as a flat. And absolutely incredible. I did that, and my response rate went up on that because I did have an offer on that one. The phone just wouldn't stop ringing. I got another 15%, 20% up on that. The key thing that this has really done for me personally is that since I've been a gold member and last year I went up to Diamond and just constantly taking everything, Mark, the house washer down in Florida, I took his ideas and twisted those around into a carpet cleaning package. Just taking every idea that's out there and putting it into my niche was tremendous. The big thing is that I've been able to now get out of carpet cleaning. I have been able to get, I still own the business, but I've been able to get off the truck, and that was my challenge to myself. I continued to set goals, and my goal for 12 months was to get off the truck and not have to do the cleaning myself anymore, which I'm happy to say I made that. My other goal was to spend more time with my family. My gosh, this has been absolutely liberating. I've got four kids, and if I can make it to every soccer game, ballet, recital, and swim meet for my kids, anybody can do this. It's just no-brainer. And finally, Dan, I went to your A to Z Info product last April. I met some incredible people there, and I have now been banned from my franchiser for teaching your style of... Oh, really, I have. They've said, don't do it. So, of course, I continue to do it. But they have said, no, we do not want you teaching our franchises this kind of marketing. It doesn't work, blah, blah, blah. I'm like, all right, whatever. I'll teach it to our competitors, but I'm going to teach it. So, I've done... I did two tele-seminars over the summer, followed Rob Scroats' book to the letter, did everything that Bill Glazer and Dan have said. And he just followed these recipes. They flip and worked. There's no reason to reinvent the wheel. Just do what they say and do it, and it works. And then last week, I did have a conference call. Actually, Dave D would be very happy. I did a Google Hangout, exactly how he said to do it, get Google Hangout. And I had over 50 franchises on the call, of which I converted seven into paying clients for my newsletter subscription, which I just started a newsletter subscription about 30 days ago. So, out of 50, I got seven to sign up for that. We're doing another one at the end of this month, and I'm hoping to get a few more. So, taking the carpet cleaning stuff, turning it into a great info product. You know, I know my friend Joe Polish out there is kind of stepped back a little bit from carpet cleaning, so there's a bit of a void. And I'm hoping to step in and fill it. Outstanding. Outstanding. So, all the right stuff. I mean, ignoring your franchisor. That's a good thing. And, you know, the biggest key, I think, to what you said is how many different funnels you have for bringing in new customers. And, of course, again, second business driven by first business. That's the mark of a great GKIC member. So, congratulations. Is there a place for people to get a glimpse of your business and your marketing? Yeah, it's not live yet, but I will put it up. And now we come last to you as usually first, Dwight, who they may, most of the people on the call may not know your business, but they certainly know you as the annoying fellow who was usually first on the call. That's me. So, it's probably a good thing that you're not competing based on votes by all the other members. So, just like with Richard and Vance, Dwight will just assume nobody knows you and you can give us the description of your business and the report on what's going on and how you've used specific things from GKIC and specifically. From your Diamond membership. Okay. All right. So Dwight Woods and I'm the chief instructor at Unified Martial Art Academy here in Miami, and we teach Bruce Lee's Jeet Kune Do. So that's Bruce Lee's martial art method and also his martial art philosophy. And I've been involved in that for about 40 years and teaching for about 30 years. In the martial art world, I'm kind of best known for being a top student of Dan Inesano, and he in turn is best known for being Bruce Lee's number one disciple. So if during the course of this presentation, you hear me say something like JKD, that's just the abbreviation that we use for Jeet Kune Do. You know, it's actually something that both you and Richard touched on there that at Glaser Kennedy, we're really good at highlighting the phenomenal successes of our members. And sometimes we think that everyone is achieving spectacular results with everything that they do. And so sometimes people are out there looking for only the big score and forgetting that success can also come from testing small things and tweaking and refining what works well for you. And so your success can be something that takes place organically over a period of time and not just the slam bang quick success. And there's also another reality that not everything that you touch is going to turn to gold. There's a reality in which most of your marketing experiments don't even work. And so you have to develop what Joe Polish calls marketing stamina and learn from the experience and you move on. And we live in a new world and sometimes people forget the old principles that never change like Clement Stone's small hinges swing big doors. So what I want to tell everybody listening today about it's about the importance of continuing to study what's being taught at GKIC and remembering to implement even if it's one thing and even if it's slowly, you're still going to come out ahead. So this is something then that you and I have talked about before that in martial art there's not going to be any major breakthroughs in what we do. That's not the nature of the game. It's like house painting is probably not going to have major breakthroughs in that industry. But my breakthrough resulted from taking something that I first learned in GKONDO, which I call the four tenets of GKONDO and which I have applied in a very different way using GKIC marketing principles. And what it's led to in just the past two months is 100% conversion rate of everyone who has taken what we call the orientation program. So I've been using it in the school, which I've run for over 20 years, and I've just started to use it as a launch pad for the information market and business, which I've only been running for about one and a half months and I actually took some time off of that after the Info Summit. So I'll focus more on the application in the school as I run you through the process, right? So like everyone else, we go for an initial consultation or discovery session, whatever you want to call it, and that leads to the trial program, which leads to the student signing up as the new student. So now let's go back to 2006, which was my first live Glazer Kennedy event, and my realization that you and Bill were teaching in marketing exactly what Bruce Lee was teaching in martial art. So the four tenants of GKINDO, tenant number one is research your own experience for the truth. And the word is right there, research, as in do your market research first. Unlike the guy that I heard you talking about who wanted to open up the gift shop in the quaint holiday town because that's what he always wanted to do, but he didn't do the research to determine that that market was oversaturated with quaint gift shops. And tenant number two is absorb what is useful, which means take a look, as is taught at GKIC, take a look at what's happening in other industries, or as Bill would say, swipe and deploy. So since 2006, I've become very interested in what dentists and chiropractors are doing. So if somebody like Tom Orent or Brian Berg or Dustin Burlson, if they're speaking, I'm listening and taking notes and I'm even on Ben Altadana's mailing list. Tenet number three is reject what is useless. And what that means to me is you test, you keep what's working, you drop what is not working, you test again, and you just keep repeating that. And I learned that way back when the Glazer-Kennedy website was an opt-in page for the success strategies emails with the picture of you sitting on the bull. And when I was a member of Peak Performers, we were actually given access to the test results for that particular webpage. So the last tenant number four is add what is specifically your own. And to me in Glazer-Kennedy parlance, that means develop your USP. Learn how to position yourself and your business both from your client's perspective, but also learning how to think differently about yourself from your own perspective. So these are the principles used. From you, I learned to get paid to pitch your advertising ideas. So we don't offer a free trial lesson or even a free week. Instead, we get paid $60 for people to try us out. I also heard from you the story of the dry cleaner having people come back three times in a row. So I designed the trial program to consist of three separate lessons, which is delayed sales approach, which is another strategy I heard you talk about. Another technique I learned from you is to apply the initial fee that the client pays to the work that you get hired to do. So we offer two opportunities to have the $60 applied to the sign up fee, either at the end of the private lesson or at the end of the first group class. From Bill, back in the days of the A to Z info blueprints, I learned the QP plus QL plus RR, which stands for risk reversal. So we offer 100% money back guarantee on the $60 for the trial program. So we remove all, there's zero risk in checking us out. From you and the price strategy, I learned how to get the price that I want, which is actually $169 a month. So we simply tell the prospect that we value what we do at $189 per month, but we realize that in this economy, $169 is more realistic. And so that's their current membership. And that's actually a rate that they can lock in regardless of what increases might occur when the economy recovers. So all of that obviously is what, you know, anybody can gain that just from being a GKIC member, but the attention to detail is what you pick up. At least that's where I picked it up, from being first in goal plus, which became Diamond, and then from spending four years in peak performers. And so it is ridiculous, I guess, for some people's standards that I am on every call or, you know, as often as I can, and that I always have a question. I say that's because I'm a slow learner, but I get specific advice that helps me to devise all these diabolical strategies that really enable me to differentiate myself in the martial art world, which is not easy to do. So that's it. That's what I've done. So tell me very quickly. Yeah. The change now that has led to 100% conversion. I think that part of it is getting better at doing the presentation, right? Removing some of the obstacles that got me in my own way. So I am not inclined to push people's pain buttons, but getting over that obstacle has resulted in them immediately assigning to me the trust that they don't have, the confidence that they don't have in themselves. They have started to assign that to me. And I think also the risk reversal aspect, I think that's probably a big deal also. But it's probably just me getting better at the presentation. All right. And where can people get a glimpse at everything you are doing? So there are actually three websites because we niche the market. So there's MiamiFitnessKickboxin.com. Then there's, I'll have to spell this one, there's Miami Muay Thai. And Muay Thai is spelled M-U-A-Y-T-H-A-I. MiamiMuayThai.com. And the third one is... I got indigestion from that the other night at Red Lobster. Well, that's because you're supposed to go to a Thai restaurant to get that, not Red Lobster. Okay. What are you working on for the new year? Okay. One thing is I'm going to convert my entire business from chasing to attraction. 100%. I'm about 70% there. So my goal is to keep working on the marketing to the point where again, I don't chase anyone. Everyone comes to me when it's getting a second goal. It's to grow the speaker book process and to see how much legs it has. I have the swing that that's going to overtake my main business. I'm already approaching some higher level people with joint ventures for it. And I've got some interest. So I want to roll that out and grow it and make it into a big product and standalone business, essentially is what I want to do with it. I also want to deepen my knowledge of marketing to where it should continually work on it. I love it. I enjoy it the more I learn the better. Those are the things I'm going to be working on this year. Very good. All right. Vance? Thanks, Dan. The goals I have are twofold for both businesses. So I've really just got two real goals and probably a whole bunch of little sub-goals underneath all of those. I wanted to ask you two, you're a Disney guy. Aren't you? You come from Disney? I do, yes, sir. Yeah. Just a minute or so. Tell folks a little bit about that. Yeah. I was with Disney for 10 years down in Orlando. I was a guest service manager for a number of years, both at Pleasure Island, which for those of you that don't know, it's not there anymore, really. That was Disney's attempt at marrying booze and adult entertainment. Yeah. It's sort of been eaten up by downtown Disney, right? Exactly. Yeah. There used to be a thing. We went, we took one group there. I used to go outside the adventurers' club. I thought it was very cool. Oh, yeah. Did you have the bar stools shrink and go up on you? Oh, yeah. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Yeah, it was great. That was a great place. But that's actually where I started doing a lot of my coaching was at Disney. We brought in a, if you can imagine, Disney brings in service consultants all the time. To talk about a big company still doing the learning, they know they don't know it all, even though a lot of people go there to learn, they still bring folks in. And we were actually, myself and a couple of Swiss hoteliers, were on the turnaround team for the contemporary resort, the one that has the Bonneville going through it. And we redid every restaurant and every concept in there with this one service guy. And I was, talk about being nervous, speaking in front of 200 or 300 other hotel and restaurant managers. And I was maybe 28 years old, so I was a bit nerve-wracking, but awesome place down there. Cool. All right. Dwight, 2014. Tribe building, both at the school and in information marketing.
